id3: Outbreak of Pandemic Covid-19 and its Management
marg: Pandemics end not when disease transmission ceases, but rather when the disease ceases to be newsworthy. The end of a pandemic is more of a concern of lived experience than of biology making it a social rather than a medical event.
sarg1: Human behaviour and interactions also affect viral spread. In India, during the monsoons, due to crowding, a spike in vector-borne diseases (dengue and malaria) is common.
sarg2: Lifestyle choices play an important role- school closures during summer and winter have been linked to reduced social contacts, and thereby a reduction in influenza cases.
sarg3: As more individuals gain immunity either through infection or vaccination (herd immunity), they indirectly offer protection to those who are not infected. The disease spread slows and eventually halts as the virus is unable to find new hosts to infect.

id3: Effectiveness of Vigilance Administration in Revenue and Banking Institutions and selected Central Public Sector Enterprises
marg: Sympathisers of anti-social elements weaken enforcement of uniform criminal laws. In such cases, cleansing of system with suitable legislation (including structural reform) helps.
sarg1: Initiatives include: Demonetisation, tax reforms, closure of shell companies, agricultural reforms, weeding out archaic laws, digitisation, Insolvency and Bankruptcy code and DBT transfers.
sarg2: Faceless income tax assessment, online submission of application for government services like passport, driving license, cashless goods and services tax, a transparent resolution mechanism under IBC, etc., have the potential to clean the system. Digitisation, for example, leaves an audit trail, thereby preventing corrupt practices.
sarg3: Structural reforms in education, agriculture, cooperative banks are second generation reforms. The implementation of these reforms is also the responsibility of the State government as these subjects are in the concurrent list. Recent agriculture reforms has been a failed endeavour in this regard.

id3: Mental Health in contemporary times
marg: Drug epidemics are not like plague of locusts or earthquakes. They afflict societies that are already in trouble.
sarg1: There is little doubt that the advanced disintegration of Qing Empire in 1840s was a precondition for the opioid epidemic that followed.
sarg2: In the American case, the most important previous opioid epidemic came during and after the Civil War. And on a smaller scale, there was widespread use of opium and heroin by American troops in Vietnam.
sarg3: The fact that the current surge in drug deaths is concentrated almost entirely among those without college degree tells us that despair and disintegration were preconditions that gave the dealers the foothold they needed.

id3: India's position in the ongoing Climate Change Negotiations
marg: One problem with trying to address climate change and debt with single package is that they do not align perfectly.
sarg1: Climate-mitigation financing is needed most in high income countries, with about one-third in Europe and the US and more than half in the Asia-Pacific region. With few exceptions, low income countries' contribution to global warming is negligible.
sarg2: Of the many low-income countries, countries such as Haiti, Niger and South Sudan face both high debts and acute climate risk, while others confront only one of these problems. No distinction exists here with respect to financing the debt distress through conditional debt relief packages offered by IMF.
sarg3: Since the 1980s, debt-for-nature swaps had not taken off. They were attractive for conservation organisations as long as they could purchase distressed debt at highly discounted prices and secure leverage for their donor funding.

id3: Inter-sectoral review of challenges of emerging and converging technologies, entities and practices
marg: Multi cloud-centric digital "outsiders" are disrupting almost every industry including financial services, healthcare, media and entertainment, retail, travel, logistics, education, consumer electronics and transportation among others.
sarg1: Some organisations turn to the cloud for short-term gains like the ability to move spending from fixed capital to ongoing operations; to receive regular technology updates without doing a lot of heavy lifting; or to upscale their technology infrastructure and applications, seamlessly.
sarg2: Most businesses choose multiple cloud providers, in part to reduce the risk of downtime and performance bottlenecks.
sarg3: The significant results and outcomes arrive to those organisations that see cloud more as a liberator, freeing their technical people from spending most of their valuable time on system security, tuning, maintenance, provisioning and upgrades. The advantage here is to invest more time on developing unique yet profitable digital products and services.

id3: Development of Tourism Infrastructure in the country
texp: The term niche may be used when a particular social group occupies a space or when a subculture occupies a specific niche within a larger community by appropriating specific places or infrastructure. Niche tourism refers to how a specific tourism product can be tailored to meet the needs of a particular audience/market segment. Niche tourism, through image creation, helps destinations to differentiate their tourism products and compete in an increasingly competitive and cluttered tourism environment. Niche tourism can take a geographical or demographic approach, product-related approach or consumer-related approach. This tourism is in contrast to mass-market approach of traditional tourism that targeted entire marketplaces, such as specific countries or regions with "one size fits all" experiences. Some examples of niche tourism include adventure tourism, bicycle tourism, sports tourism, gaming tourism, pro-poor tourism, or geo tourism.

id3: Issues related to safety of Women
texp: Sexism is linked to power in that those with power are typically treated with favour and those without power are typically discriminated against. Sexist assumptions about women and men, which manifest themselves as gender stereotypes, can rank one gender as superior to another. Such hierarchical thinking can be conscious and hostile, or it can be unconscious, manifesting itself as unconscious bias. Rank Sexism can touch everyone, but women are particularly affected. When a man calls a woman a girl, he places his association in that relationship as either father or boy. Clearly, what is at issue is power. A woman is a female who has a sense of her own identity and personal power, as well as a sense of her sexuality. A girl is an immature female, a child, whose sexuality is not threatening. Rank Sexism is also related to stereotypes since discriminatory actions or attitudes are frequently based on false beliefs or generalisations about gender, and on considering gender as relevant where it is not.

id3: Effective Implementation of Labour Laws concerning the welfare of contract/casual labour
texp: Occupational licensing is the process by which governments establish qualifications required to practice a trade or profession, so that only licensed practitioners are allowed by law to receive pay for doing work in the occupation. In 1960s and 1970s, majority of the licensed occupations comprised of doctors, dentists and lawyers. Presently, certain licensed occupations are turning into glorified guilds. To be a certified chartered accountant in India, one must have three years of articled training and pass an examination. Also, occupational licensing places limits on the activities that the licensees are legally allowed to perform as part of that profession such as demonstrating good moral character, working with or for local practitioners, and engaging in ongoing professional development activities (an investment that continues throughout the worker’s career). Sometimes, it could also hamper mobility, acting as a deterrent to geographical movement. Contractual work comes along with "non-compete" clauses preventing workers from easily leaving their jobs for high paying ones.

id3: Policy issues in Information Technology including cross border data flows, Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Internet of Things , (IoT), etc.
nws: A policy proposal titled "Draft India Data Accessibility and Use Policy" had been recently released.
pnt1: It aims to “radically transform” India’s ability to harness public sector data for large-scale social transformation. It will be applicable to all data and information created, generated and collected by the government directly or through Ministries, Departments and authorised agencies.
pnt2: The draft policy outlines an institutional framework entailing India Data Office, India Data Council and Data Management Units as key components.
pnt3: State governments will be free to adopt the provisions and protocols as applicable.
pnt4: Minimally processed data sets will be made freely available. Only detailed data sets that have undergone value addition/transformation and qualify for monetisation will be priced appropriately.
pnt5: For restricted access data sharing, pricing of datasets as decided by the owner government department or agency must be notified in a transparent manner.

id3: Ayurveda System of Medicine: Challenges and Prospects
nws: The incidence of 'dieback disease' in Neem is said to be very high this year.
pnt1: Dieback refers to the progressive death of twigs and branches which generally starts at the tips. It gradually leads to the death of the tree.
pnt2: The disease changes the leaf colour to pale green or yellow, scorches the leaf margins and reduces the growth of twigs and stem.
pnt3: Though there is no estimate of yields , Neem extract is widely used in remedial formulations.
pnt4: The disease was first reported in India in Dehradun in 1992.

More clarity will be required to define ‘high value’ data sets as broader terms like “based on importance in the market”, “socio economic benefits” etc, which provide for wider powers to the incoming institutional body (Data council) to decide on these things won’t suffice. Also, we need to create strong processes to minimise hurdles in the standardisation of prices. The GDPR has some of the best practices of data collection and processing, and mentions that data should be collected for a specific purpose and should be kept only to fulfil the purpose and not be further processed. The moment data collection is incentivised, it becomes problematic and can go against purpose limitation and end up impacting data privacy. Strict access control is needed for proper anonymisation. It’s very easy to re-identify any data even after proper anonymisation.

id3: Implementation of Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) mandates
opn1: The expanded disclosure requirements demand an unprecedented level of granular detail- on ongoing and new projects, what funds have been left unspent, and whether capital assets have been created, impact assessment reports, constitution of the company’s CSR committee, the details of the committee, and approved CSR projects on the company’s website. More concerning, is the direction that the CSR mandate has taken. What started out as a comply-or-explain template for large companies under the United Progressive Alliance government has evolved into a quasi-legal commitment under its successor. In 2019, for instance, the government proposed to amend the law to treat non-compliance as a criminal offence. Though this amendment was later softened to a civil offence in 2021 after industry lobbies protested, the transition from a discretionary regime to a mandatory one had become explicit.
opnn: On Corporate Social Responsibility disclosure norms

id3: Review of long-term public finances and debt at Centre and States
opn1: According to Article 293 (3) of the Constitution, without the consent of the government of India, states may not raise any loan, as long as "there is still outstanding any part of a loan which has been made to the state by the Government of India". Thus, state borrowings are circumscribed  by the Centre's approval. But over the past decade, the Union government has done away with the loan component in the Central plan's assistance. States' liabilities towards the Centre has been declining as per the 15th Finance Commission. But, the treatment of loans from multilateral organisations to states being routed through the Centre endows the Centre with greater sway over states' fiscal management. And, these loans are treated as capital expenditure as compared to grants which is a part of revenue expenditure.

id3: Edible Oils
opn1: The government needs to focus on measures such as a targeted approach, crop diversification, recalibration of minimum support price (MSP) and providing a technological push to increase edible oil production in the country. opn1: Although India’s reliance on Russia has been declining, Russia still accounts for most of India's arms imports. An analysis of data from Stockholm International Peace Research Institute (SIPRI) shows 49.4 per cent of India’s arms imports between 2016 and 2020 were from Russia, and 0.5 per cent were from Ukraine. In the previous five years, between 2011-15, Russia accounted for 69.6 per cent of India's total arms imports. In 1996-2010, the share was 70 per cent. On average a month, India imported 200,000-225,000 tonnes of sunflower oil, of which almost 70 per cent came from Ukraine and 20 per cent from Russia while the balance 10 per cent from Argentina.

opn1:
Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunications (SWIFT) connects more than 11,000 financial institutions around the world. 
It is viewed as a potential nuclear option in the world of sanctions because, blocking Russia from SWIFT, would essentially sever the nation from much of the global financial system. It would curb Russia's ability to conduct international financial transactions by forcing importers, exporters and banks to find new ways to transmit payment instructions. Because of Europe’s heavy reliance on Russian energy exports, there is a reluctance among some euro area leaders to take that step and risk those purchases by making doing business with Russia more costly and complicated.

id3: Others
nws: The Centre is to hold peace talks with all Kuki militant groups and resolve their issues in the next five years.
pnt1: Kuki tribe is an ethnic group native to the Mizo Hills (formerly Lushai) in Mizoram. In northeast India, they are present in all states except Arunachal Pradesh.
pnt2: For both Naga and Kuki nationalisms, the First World War was a watershed moment. During the War, the British was recruiting thousands of people from its empires to assist them in the war effort. The Nagas, were pressed into service as part of the Imperial Army’s Labour Corps, a vast body of men recruited to build roads, carry luggage and provide other kinds of logistical support. This brought together different Naga tribes, many of whom had previously been at war with one another, and helped consolidate a pan-Naga identity. For the Kukis, World War 1 was crucial because of their refusal to join the Labour Corps. Instead, they declared war on the colonisers in 1917. The conflict that followed lasted for two years and claimed many lives. It has been variously called the “Great Kuki Rebellion”, the “Kuki Rising”, the “Anglo-Kuki War” or the “Zou Gaal”.
pnt3: Kuki resistance first emerged in the 18th century, according to Haokip, when fighters from the hill tribe attacked British subjects in the Chittagong Hill Tracts. It intensified after the Treaty of Yandabo, signed in 1826 and bringing to an end the First Anglo-Burmese War. Under the Treaty, the Burmese signed away large tracts of what is now India’s North East, including Assam, Manipur and the hill areas, to the British. There were periodic Kuki raids on British subjects after that, in 1845, 1847-48, 1849, 1850 and 1851. In the “Great Kuki Invasion of Tipperah” in the 1860s, 15 villages were burnt and around 185 British subjects killed.

id3: Indias preparedness for 5G
texp: Quantum Key Distribution Link (QKD) is primarily a mechanism to undertake secure communication which utilizes a cryptographic protocol involving various components of quantum mechanics. The technology enables two communicating sides to come up with random secret keys shared by both of them and known exclusively to them, so only they can use it to encrypt and decrypt messages, thus achieving a very highly-secure communication. In QKD, encryption keys are sent as ‘qubits’ (or quantum bits) in an optical fibre. The security of QKD stems from the ability to detect any intrusion on the QKD transmission. Because of the unique and fragile properties of photons, any third party (or eavesdropper) who tries to read or copy the photons in any way will change the photons’ state. The change will be detected by the endpoints, alerting them that the key has been tampered with and must be discarded. A new key is then transmitted. Moreover, since the keys generated are truly random, they are protected from future hacking attempts.

pnt2: A desire to return to the ocean goes back millennia, with evidence of sea burial in ancient Egypt and Rome. In the South Pacific, bodies would be placed in canoes and pushed out to sea, while scattering ashes in the ocean has long been widely practised in Asia.

opn1: The most sought after international destinations- traditionally, for medical education the U.S., the U.K. and a few west European countries- are, however, too expensive for most Indians. In the last few decades, Russia, China (countries larger than India) and Ukraine (one of Europe’s largest countries), with their historical commitment to public health care have been able to offer more affordable, yet quality, education. The very nature of medical education, an empirical field, requires significant infrastructure- land, equipment, and trained faculty at the post graduate level- all of which are in short supply and uneven in their spread. Without correcting these deficiencies, India cannot expect to dramatically increase the availability of medical personnel. Merely having private establishments start medical schools, without a long-term commitment to offer necessary training and postgraduate education, could lead to a repeat of the engineering fiasco after the dot com boom: a surfeit of engineering colleges without adequately trained faculty or infrastructure that churn out students who need a further skills upgrade to be employable. The Government must instead ease procedures for establishing medical colleges, spend more on infrastructure, and provide incentives for a healthcare ecosystem to develop in rural areas. This could, in the long run, facilitate the growth of private and public medical colleges that could stem the outflow of students aspiring to be doctors. Private investment in medical education by itself will nowhere be enough.
opnn: On the dynamics of India’s medical education system

id3: Functioning of Agricultural Credit System in the Country
opn1: Notwithstanding the growth in the Farmer Producer Organisations model which is based on the produce cluster area and specialised commodity approach, individual farmers find it difficult to get credit from banks and remain dependent on local money lenders who charge exorbitant interest rates. Close to 90% of the lending for FPOs come from the Non-Banking Financial Sector. The RBI needs to issue a a directive requesting banks to report their lending to FPOs and provide those NBFCs who have shown that FPOs are bankable to get priority sector lending. Other problems plaguing FPOs include lack of professional management, weak internal governance, under-capitalisation to take up activities and also absorb credit, inadequate credit linkages, access to market and inadequate access to infrastructure. Further, the growth of FPOs across states have been uneven, limiting its' success.

id3: Police - Training, Modernisation and Reforms
nws: The maximum utilization of technology with an objective to improve service delivery system and ensuring safety and security of people is essential in the present environment.
pnt1:
To ensure the safety and security of people with maximum use of technology, the Delhi Police has recently launched three digital initiatives i.e. Anubhuti- QR Code-based feedback system, Delhi Police website, and e-Chittha portal.
1. Anubhuti - Feedback Management System is a paperless feedback system. The citizens visiting the police station have to scan the QR code of the police station and mark the feedback responses. Response database will be utilized to analyze and take corrective steps for improvement in the police-public interface. The identity of the user will not be disclosed. This will establish two-way communication with the public and improve the police working through the analysis of feedback received.
2. The user-friendly and more informative website of Delhi Police will be quite useful for citizens to know about Delhi Police services with one click. 
3. e-Chittha is the backbone of duty deployment. Implementation of e-Chittha will not only ensure optimal use of manpower resources but also increase efficiency and transparency in working. The staff will get sufficient time for family and their own health and well-being and would work with more zeal and enthusiasm.
pnt2: The merging of Police Control Room into districts has resulted in the availability of more manpower and patrol vehicles at beat and police stations.
pnt3: Separation of law & order and investigation at all police stations had also necessitated equal distribution of work to bring about the desired results.

id3: Promotion of Electronics/IT Hardware/telecom equipment manufacturing sector under Make in India and measures for reduction of imports
opn1: From being a net importer of electronic goods two decades ago, India’s export of electronic goods has reached an all-time high of $12,400 million in this fiscal year ~ 88 per cent more than what it was in 2014. The National Policy on Electronics 2019 aimed to position India as a global hub for Electronics System Design and Manufacturing (ESDM) by encouraging and driving capabilities in the country for developing core components and creating an enabling environment for the industry to compete globally. Currently, the government has introduced four schemes namely- the production linked incentive scheme (PLI) for large-scale electronics manufacturing, scheme for promotion of manufacturing of electronic components and semiconductors (SPECS), modified electronics manufacturing clusters scheme (EMC 2.0) and the production linked incentive scheme (PLI) for IT hardware to give boost to the electronics sector and establish the necessary ecosystem.

id3: Implementation of The Real Estate (Regulation & Development) Act, 2016 (RERA) in urban areas-A Review
opn1: The Indian real estate sector has demonstrated great resilience during the pandemic. The housing market has bounced well and certain pockets such as Hyderabad, Ahmedabad, Delhi NCR and Pune are poised for favourable housing sales. The commercial real estate sector has also started picking up well. With the data centres being granted an infrastructure status, land pockets such as Navi Mumbai, which have emerged as hubs for data centre, also stand to gain from this current climate.

id3: Tax policies and compliance with capital controls
opn1: In 2019-20, Indians made about Rs.95,000 crore of long-term capital gains. About 80 per cent of those long-term capital gains were made by the people earning Rs.50 lakh and more per annum. In 2022-23, this share may reach 90 per cent. These are just 0.1 or 0.2 per cent of the population. Giving relief in the case of LTCG will only increase money in the pockets of a few people. Other countries charge long-term capital gains tax at the applicable rate of 25 to 30 per cent while in India it is only at 10 per cent. And the long-term period is 12 months.
opnn: On justification for Long-term Capital Gains tax

id3: Ease of Doing Business
opn1: About 40 per cent of raw material for cement production- limestone- is naturally available in the South, and there is no ordinary portland cement in the North. Unless cement moves from South to North, there will be a shortage of cement in the North. Logistics support for the cement industry in the South by way of railway freight to transport cement to northern regions will be helpful to avoid cartelisation of the industry.
opnn: On logistics support for cement sector

id3: Indias preparedness for 5G
opn1: Digital will increasingly determine competitiveness of not only companies but nations and continents. 5G will underpin the next wave of digital innovation and industrial revolution for every country and society. Every country and region will try to maximise their chance to capture this opportunity for jobs and growth. Africa needs digital to accelerate development and diversify its economies. Europe needs digital to remain globally competitive and maintain its leadership role in key sectors such as automotive, aerospace defence and agriculture.

id3: Functioning of Agricultural Credit System in the Country
opn1: NABARD has created close to 16,000 FPOs since 2010-11. The philosophy behind this concept of producers’ group is what to produce, how to produce, how much to produce and at what rates it should be sold. Many of the FPOs are now either input or output aggregators and not doing enough for the welfare of the farmers. To overcome the challenge of lower productivity, FPOs need to leverage new technologies such as artificial intelligence (AI) and Block chain towards optimising collectivisation benefits. The concept from cooperatives to collectivisation to FPOs though all in similar veins, there are differences in approach. The FPO model has the potential to enhance global trade of Indian geographical indication (GI) products at competitive prices.

id3: India's position in the ongoing Climate Change Negotiations
opn1: Only 7 per cent of all disaster events were drought-related between 1970 and 2019, yet they contributed disproportionately to 34 per cent of disaster-related death, mostly in Africa. Climate change and rising demand mean about 40 per cent of people in India will live with water scarcity by 2050 compared with about 33 per cent now. Both the Ganges and the Brahmaputra river basins will also see increased flooding as a result of climate change, particularly if warming exceeds 1.5° C. In India, rice production can decrease by 10 to 30 per cent and maize 25 to 70 per cent assuming a temperature increase of 1° to 4° C. By mid-21st century, the international trans-boundary river basins of Amu Darya, Indus, Ganges and inter-State Sabarmati river basin in India could face severe water scarcity challenges with climate change acting as a stress multiplier.
opnn: On impact of Climate Change in India

id3: Inter-sectoral review of challenges of emerging and converging technologies, entities and practices
opn1: NFT is a unique digital identifier that cannot be copied, substituted, or subdivided. It is recorded in a blockchain and is used to certify authenticity and ownership. NFTs aid in the commercialization of media and entertainment. On the Metaverse interface, media companies can generate and sell digital assets, providing an extra revenue stream. This rewards creators by providing them with a new source of income from their work.  Metaverse would trigger interactive storytelling. To design immersive experiences, creators and brand builders will need to collaborate with technologists to make ideas come alive.

id3: Status of Aviation Connectivity in the country
opn1: India’s ambitious plan to connect smaller towns and cities to the wider domestic air travel network under the government’s flagship Regional Connectivity Scheme (RCS) is struggling to take off even after five years. Also, the penetration of helicopters in India is negligible. Regional airlines are struggling to survive due to reasons such as delays in operationalization and discontinuation of various regional routes due to the non-readiness of civil airports and heliports, the unsustainability of operations on certain routes, and the adverse impact of the pandemic on passenger demand on these routes. This industry has been one of the worst-hit by the pandemic and related restrictions on mobility. This is in addition to high operating costs, especially due to rising oil prices.

id3: Promotion of Electronics/IT Hardware/telecom equipment manufacturing sector under Make in India and measures for reduction of imports
opn1: The dependence of leading global semiconductor manufacturers on raw materials, palladium and neon, sourced from Russia and Ukraine, respectively, could prolong the global semiconductor crisis and even indirectly impact device manufacturers in countries such as India because of a delay in supply of critical parts. Ukraine supplies more than 90% of the US’s semiconductor-grade neon, while Russia provides 35% of the US’s palladium. The noble gas Neon is used in critical laser lithography in semiconductor manufacturing. This process is used to etch patterns on silicon, which is fundamental to making computer chips. Palladium is used for the pins that conduct electricity on a chip. India largely draws its supplies from Taiwan and Russia.
opnn: On dependence of global semiconductor industry on Russia and Ukraine

id3: Long term impact of the Covid-19 pandemic on the global economy, trade flows, and financial markets
opn1: The Ukraine conflict has brought down the Russian currency, Ruble as well as the Ukrainian currency, Hyrvina. The conflict has highlighted the danger of the single currency- single country exposure to the Indian investors who have most of their investments in domestic stocks and bonds. Political, economic, and military issues can cause the value of investments in just the particular country and currency to drop sharply. Any period of financial crisis resulting from country-specific reasons is likely to see a correction in the stock markets as well as a simultaneous weakness in the currency. In such a period, if any investments are held outside the home country, they can protect an investor’s portfolio to an extent- since the foreign investments may not be impacted.
opnn: On the need for diversification of an individual's investment portfolio

id3: Others
opn1: Germany's active participation- including participating in the financial sanctions and suspending the Nord Stream 2 project, is a significant development, for the country firmly turned its back on nationalism as militarism after the trauma of the Hitlerian years and World War II. The evolution of Germany’s idea of its post-war military from self-defence to humanitarian intervention to multilateral participation in expeditionary wars, took place against a background of massive public protests against even rearming for national defence, against the nuclearisation of German territory during the Cold War, and in the 1990s, against participation in the Iraq and Yugoslav crisis. German airbases and finances were available for the 1990 Operation Desert Storm, but not soldiers, and during the war in the former Yugoslavia, Germany contributed to enforcing an embargo at sea against Serbia and Montenegro. German public opinion also underwent a shift after the Serbian army carried out a massacre of Bosnian Muslims at Srebrenica. Germany’s leadership at the turn of the century enabled the country’s participation along with other NATO countries in the US-led Afghan war in 2001. It was based on the rationale that Europe’s economic powerhouse simply could not sit out a war against terrorism, being waged for “everything our world holds dear”, including freedom, democracy, tolerance and human life.
opnn: On Germany's active participation in countering Russian aggression

id3: India's Soft Power and Cultural Diplomacy: Prospects & Limitations
opn1: Sport is finally pulling its weight in international affairs, and proving to live up to the cliche of being a pathfinder for the youth. Russia has been hit by economic sanctions, and freezing of its financial assets. But nothing visibly isolates a football-mad nation such as Russia like the refusal to engage with it on a football field. The invasion has brought the spotlight on Russian oligarchs and their ownership of clubs, notably Chelsea, which will serve as another moral and monetary leverage as the rest of the world attempts to stop Russia. Sport upon sport- from gymnastics to badminton- have issued strong condemnations, revoking of hosting rights to Russian cities and ordered non-display of Russian flags wherever their athletes participate. The most poignant opposition to the war has come from Russian big names on the international circuit. While Andrey Rublev scribbled ‘No War’ on camera after winning at Dubai, a host of Russian chess GMs — Ian Nepomniachtchi, Peter Svidler and Alexandra Kosteniuk — have ventured to take a stand against war, calling it a dark day.

id3: Achieving $ 10 Trillion GDP – Long-term prospects for the Indian Economy
opn1: A historical preference towards investments in heavy industries with significant global competition over labour intensive sectors has slowed down India's growth. Some states will have to take the lead to allow "hire and fire" for large factories. India would have to become open to benefit fully from reforms such as Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, labour law reforms, GST and the corporate tax cut. When you restrict imports, you also restrict exports. Modern supply chains are constituted of large firms specialising in single activities done on a global scale and participation in such supply chains get hampered by even 5 percent tariff as these goods moved through multiple countries. Protectionism today hurts you a lot more today than it did in the past because inputs have to cross many borders. Most major supply chains in Asia are in countries with free trade agreements with goods moving without barriers.

id3: Ensuring Quality of Milk and Consumer Grievance Redressal Mechanism in Dairy Sector
opn1: Appointment of a private diary company representative as one of the directors of the National Dairy Development Board would lead the NDDB being controlled by market forces. On January 18, the Union government issued a draft notification to amend the NDDB Act, 1987. According to the proposal, the NDDB can have two directors, one of whom shall be from private dairy industries. Presently, NDDB acts as a nodal agency for funding, implementing, and monitoring dairy development activities. Since Aavin relies on NDDB for infrastructure improvements, the latter should not be allowed to be headed by private players.
opnn: On the proposal to appoint a private dairy company representative as one of the directors of NDDB

id3: Potential of Tourist Spots in the country - Connectivity and Outreach
nws: The government is to hand over about 1,000 notified heritage sites and monuments to private players or non-government organisations for maintenance under a special arrangement such as the Monument Mitra (adopt a heritage) scheme.
pnt1: The Monument Mitra project was envisioned to provide an enhanced tourism experience and also to ensure quality and inclusive provision of amenities and facilities at heritage sites.
pnt2: The government has signed MoUs with private entities for more than 24 sites including Dara Shikoh Library Building (Delhi), Aguada Fort (Goa), Bara Lao ka Gumbad (Delhi), Rani-ki-Vav (Gujarat), Tomb of Abdur Rahim Khan-i-Khana (Delhi), Gandikota Fort (Andhra Pradesh), Red Fort (Delhi), and Ajanta Caves (Maharashtra).

id3: Augmenting Infrastructure Facilities to Boost Export
opn1: PM Gati Shakti will ensure true public-private participation in infrastructure creation from infrastructure planning to development and utilisation stage. There is lack of coordination among the stakeholders in the traditional way of completing projects. This was due to the lack of clear information among various departments. With PM Gati Shakti everyone will be able to make their plan with complete information. This will give a new direction to infrastructure planning, implementation and monitoring, and also reduce time and cost overrun of projects.

id3: National Hydrogen Energy Mission
nws: International hydrogen trade needs to start soon if it is to make an impact on the global energy system.
pnt1: Make industrial ports the nerve centres for scaling up the use of clean hydrogen: Today, much of the refining and chemicals production that uses hydrogen based on fossil fuels is already concentrated in coastal industrial zones around the world. Encouraging these plants to shift to cleaner hydrogen production would drive down overall costs. These large sources of hydrogen supply can also fuel ships and trucks serving the ports and power other nearby industrial facilities like steel plants.
pnt2: Build on existing infrastructure, such as millions of kilometres of natural gas pipelines: Introducing clean hydrogen to replace just 5% of the volume of natural gas supplies would significantly boost demand for hydrogen and drive down costs.
pnt3: Expand hydrogen in transport through fleets, freight and corridors: Powering high-mileage cars, trucks and buses to carry passengers and goods along popular routes can make fuel-cell vehicles more competitive.
pnt4: Launch the hydrogen trade's first international shipping routes: Lessons from the successful growth of the global LNG market can be leveraged.

id3: India's position in the ongoing Climate Change Negotiations
opn1: Fossil fuels not only cause climate change, but also enable warfare in the midst of multiple crises in which we all live. On February 24, 2021, the Russian Army invaded Ukraine to start a full-scale war in the centre of Europe. In 2019, 41% of EU natural gas imports came from Russia. Russia has been a major emitter of fossil fuels and a contributor to climate change. We see today how fossil fuel dependency and conflict are intertwined. Fossil fuels are a dead end- for our planet, for humanity, and yes, for economies.
opnn: On correlation between fossil-fuel dependency and conflict

id3: Mental Health in contemporary times
opn1: The arrest of the school headmistress for abetment of suicide in the student’s suicide in Faridabad, as questionable as Rhea Chakraborty being similarly booked in 2020. The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 recognised that attempt to suicide is not a crime- both logically and medically driving people to suicide should not be a crime either. Indian schools are crying for structural protections against bullying and fixing accountability for excesses is also important. Only 3% of private schools in Delhi-NCR have counsellors, as against the CBSE guideline requiring at least one full-time counsellor per school. But populist scapegoating is a poor substitute and does little to prevent a recurrence of the tragedy.
opnn: On arrest of school headmistress on charges of abetment of suicide

id3: Others
Social Security and Welfare measures for workers in unorganized and informal sectors
opn1: Cross-sector collaboration and multidisciplinary approach is essential for dynamic and agile regulation for disruptive but  exciting telecom technologies. Digital inclusion needs to be the highest priority, especially for women and the marginalised, small enterprises and informal economy. For them, digital economy can be a boon. But they require handholding in the form of affordable connectivity, applications and use cases, especially in light of 5G-based Industry 4.0 technologies. Digital penetration has also raised issues of data protection, privacy and cyber security.

id3: New Unified Structure pertaining to Cyber Space, Space and Special Operations
opn1: Spacecom is emerging the new disruptor in telecommunications. Satellite communications will be able to reach far-flung areas, difficult areas, and, in a quicker timeframe, provide not only telephony but also broadband. Space communication will not be replacing terrestrial communication - it is a co-traveller, enhancing the capacity required to make digital India possible. Satellite telecommunications can also be used for terrestrial backhaul.

id3: Strengthening RBI and SEBI’s regulatory and operational capabilities
nws: RBI's harmonised regulatory framework for regulated lenders, "RBI (Regulatory Framework for Microfinance Loans) Directions, 2022" will be effective from April 1, 2022.
pnt1: All collateral-free loans, irrespective of end-use and mode of application/ processing/ disbursal, provided to low-income households- with annual income up to ₹3 lakh- are considered microfinance loans.
pnt2: Interest rates and other charges/fees on these loans will be subject to supervisory scrutiny by the RBI.
pnt3: Any change in interest rate or any other charge will have to be informed to the borrower well in advance.
pnt4: The Regulated Entities (scheduled commercial banks, small finance banks, NBFC-MFIs and NBFC-Investment and Credit Companies) or its agent shall not engage in any harsh methods towards loan recovery.

id3: Import of Coal – Trends and Issue of Self Reliance
opn1: India's demand for coking coal, used in steel making, is growing at a faster clip , and the country imports the bulk of its' requirements from Australia. Overall coking coal imports total 50-55 million tonnes, with overseas purchases rising 4 per cent annually. To reduce its import dependence on Australia, India, in 2021, agreed with Russia to import coking coal, which accounts for about 40 per cent of the total cost of steel production.
opnn: On rocketing prices of coal in the wake of Russia's invasion of Ukraine

id3: India's position in the ongoing Climate Change Negotiations
opn1: It is hard to miss the preponderance of solar in renewables installations in recent years, but to triple its non-fossil fuel power in nine years, India has to look beyond solar. Wind is an obvious candidate, with an estimated potential of 700 GW at a hub height of 120 meters. But, offshore wind power is too expensive- around ₹8 a kWhr- essentially due to high installation costs. Large hydro, whose potential, estimated at 145 GW, is thrice as much as the existing capacity, has been hampered mainly by environmental concerns and rehabilitation and resettlement issues. Nuclear power , capacity today stands at a dismal 6,780 MW. The major problem is the ‘liability clause’, which has scared away reactor suppliers. Small modular reactors (SMR) are getting global attention, though none has been built. These can be sited at places vacated by retired thermal plants. India- indeed the world- has ignored ocean energies. These are expensive, like offshore wind, but costs can be tamed with scale. Oceans, like large hydro and nuclear, can provide 24x7 base load energy.

id3: Import of fertilizers and the issue of self dependence
opn1: Nano urea and promotion of natural farming will substantially bring down current usage of urea in bags. The government has also talked about green ammonia which is the next thing to help reduce urea. As dosage will be very low leading to surplus and also helping balanced use of fertiliser, the government should simultaneously prepare to capture the market vacated by China. China, in 2021, banned export of urea to meet domestic demand, and as a result, global prices flared up to record high. Recently, Russia also restricted outbound shipments amid the war in Ukraine and subsequent sanctions by US and European Union.

id3: Inter-sectoral review of challenges of emerging and converging technologies, entities and practices
opn1: Big NLP is really important to India, as it’s a large model that can take billions of parameters into account and do what human brains cannot. Conversational AI is extremely critical at call centres and on websites. These services need to be augmented so that we can go beyond English to multiple global and local languages. Our nation is multilingual with thousands of dialects, and we need to find a solution for the community. A lot of such work is taking shape in India.
opnn: On scope and opportunity for natural language processing in India

id3: Self-reliance in Minerals and Metals
nws: Nickel prices have doubled in the past month and trebled in the past year.
pnt1: 81 per cent of nickel ores and concentrates of Europe’s imports come from Russia. Specifically, the European Union gets 30 per cent of its nickel imports from Russia. Exchange tradeable and deliverable nickel is only 25 per cent of the nickel that can be sold globally. Post commencement of Russia-Ukraine conflict Russia has banned exports of nickel and the metal’s trading was affected due to sanctions announced by the US, Europe and their allies against Kremlin.
pnt2: Nickel can alloy with steels, irons, coppers, chromium, aluminum, lead, cobalt, silver, gold and other elements to make stainless steels, cast irons, inconel, incoloy, monel, nimonic and other alloys.
pnt3: Nickel is used in making stainless steel, other alloys, batteries, electronics and medical device among others. Nickel alloys function as high performance alloys, corrosion resistant alloys, electronic alloys, and heat resistant alloys.

id3: Review of cyber security scenario in India
nws: So far, seven cases of money laundering using cryptocurrency have been registered in India.
pnt1: The crypto-currencies’ attractions to money launderers are global availability, the speed and irreversibility of transactions and the ability to hide identities.
pnt2: Dirty cash- from drug-dealing, say- can be washed by converting it into crypto, splitting it into smaller amounts and moving it through the crypto-sphere, perhaps via several virtual currencies. Dirty crypto, for example from a ransomware attack, can be similarly swapped around- often at high speed (“atomic swaps”) and in little chunks (“micro laundering”)- until it is clean enough to be switched into ordinary money.
pnt3: The global anti-money laundering system has serious structural flaws, largely because governments have outsourced to the private sector much of the policing they should have been doing themselves.
pnt4: The Covid-19 pandemic, has boosted opportunities for "trade-based money laundering". Criminals have set up shell companies to exploit vast, poorly policed government-aid schemes.

id3: Issues Related to Improving Consumers’ Satisfaction of Airlines
opn1: Air travel must not be treated as a contractual matter between the airline and the passenger. Fixing of different fares for selection of seats in the same flight is arbitrary and unjustifiable. Hence, on the principle of equity, all the seats in a flight should have same fare. Passengers should not be fleeced in the garb of commercialisation. A balance must be maintained between the commercial interests of the airlines and the interest of the passengers.
opnn: On private airlines' ticket pricing policy

id3: Rejuvenation of forests base
opn1: Miyawaki forests are not natural forests. They do not have undergrowth, the girth of trees does not expand, and people cannot enter these forests. In Miyawaki method, the trees are artificially selected. They are not based on local conditions. They are then subjected to competition and stress. Therefore, they lose their natural characteristics. For example, Neem trees grow like pine trees. Also, these trees cannot play ecological services due to lack of space. This will not help a place like Tamil Nadu that has many native species.

id3: Strengthening the Justice Delivery process
opn1: All rights have to be viewed in the contextual conditions which were framed under the Constitution and the way in which they have evolved in the due course. The Fundamental Rights have relative content and their efficacy levels depend upon the circumstances in which they are sought to be exercised.
opnn: On the need to contextualise rights

id3: Development of Deep Sea Fishing
opn1: Purse seine fishing is a non-targeted fishing which is popular on India’s western coasts. It uses a large vertical net to surround dense shoals of pelagic or midwater fish in the open ocean and then draws in the edges like tightening the cords of a drawstring purse. It has led to a decrease in pelagic shoaling fish such as sardines, mackerel, anchovies and trevally on the western coast. Fishing is a 'State' subject. Currently, the ban on purse seine fishing is implemented in the territorial waters of Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Puducherry, Odisha, Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu, and the Andaman and Nicobar Islands up to 12 nautical miles. States such as Gujarat, Andhra Pradesh, Goa, Karnataka, and West Bengal have not imposed any such ban, while Maharashtra has issued some orders to regulate, but not ban purse seine fishing in its territorial waters. Rather than banning, the government should bring in certain regulations. Automatic identification systems can be established to identify the boats engaged in such fishing.

id3: Flood Management in the country with special reference to States of Bihar, Uttar Pradesh and West Bengal
pnt1: Stormwater management is the effort to reduce the runoff of rainwater or melted snow into streets, lawns and other sites and the improvement of water quality.
pnt3: Facilitating stormwater infiltration and storage facilities under street drains at specific points to reduce flooding on roads.
pnt4: Using abandoned farm ponds, school playgrounds and parking spaces to store floodwater.
pnt5: Providing subsidies for installing facilities to store floodwater (Demand Control approach)

id3: India's Extended Neighbourhood: From Look East to Act East
nws: Mizoram has an influx of Myanmar nationals crossing it since the country's coup in February 2021.
pnt1: 23 Sector Assam Rifles is responsible for guarding and managing 510 km of the India-Myanmar border of the total 1,643 km.
pnt2: The border is porous, the major portion is riverine border and rugged terrain with large number of unreachable (no go) areas.
pnt3: Special provision of Free Movement Regime (FMR) along with ethnic affiliation makes dynamics of the border unique and challenging.
pnt4: The limited availability of troops, proximity to the Golden Triangle, FMR, an unstable Myanmar, the boundary issue between Mizoram and Assam, transit point of Indian insurgent groups and smuggling of narcotics and contraband makes the security situation in Mizoram very dynamic.

id3: Conservation and Preservation of Forests and National Parks
opn1: Orans or sacred groves in Jaisalmer region of Rajasthan face the threat of destruction as lands are allotted for construction of renewable energy infrastructure and high-tension power lines. Orans form the natural habitat for India’s most critically endangered bird, the Great Indian Bustard (GIB). Many birds have died during the last few years because of collision with power lines.
opnn: On protection of orans (sacred groves) in Rajasthan
